text: Robotic revision of hepatico-gigenostomy. We present here a patient who had a history of chronic pancreatitis and benign biliary stricture. The patient underwent numerous endoscopic procedures and ERCPs approximately ten years ago, but ultimately required a surgical biliary bypass procedure. This was done in an outside facility in two thousand and nine and the patient did well for several years. In the past several months the patient began developing recurring episodes of cholangitis. Imaging revealed multiple intrahepatic stones and biliary dilatation consistent with a stricture at the previous choledochogigenostomy.

The surgeon uses robotic monopolar scissors to dissect and amputate the old anastomosis site, revealing vegetable and other debris within the roux limb of the small intestines. The distal aspect of the bile duct is carefully dissected from surrounding adhesions in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully dissect around structures in the porta hepatis, entering the dilated segment of the bile duct through the stricture. Approximately two centimeters of the bile duct are amputated, and the old cystic artery is identified and clipped, facilitating the removal of the strictured bile duct.

The surgeon sends the bile duct margin for frozen section analysis to rule out malignancy. The existing Roux limb in the right upper quadrant is mobilized to maximize its length without reconstructing the entire limb. The old anastomosis site is closed using a running absorbable barbed suture in a single layer.

The surgeon pexies the roux limb to the anterior wall of the stomach to prevent retraction. Using monopolar scissors, a new enterotomy is created for the anastomosis, which is at least one centimeter in diameter. The anastomosis is initiated with a running absorbable monofilament barbed suture on the posterior wall, starting at the nine o'clock position and running medially towards the three o'clock position. Another suture is started at the nine o'clock position and run in a forehand fashion along the anterior wall. The robotic platform facilitates skill transfer to a trainee, visible with blue cones indicating faculty supervision.
The surgeon completes the revisional hepaticojejunostomy by tying together the two cut ends of the absorbable suture. A drain is left in place, although it is later removed.
